title: Why Authentication?
intro: Your task manager works great locally, but once it goes online, anyone could read or delete anyone else's tasks. Let's explore why authentication matters and what it actually protects.

teach.title: What Is Authentication?
teach.body: Authentication is how your app answers the question 'Who are you?' When a user logs in, the app verifies their identity and then uses that identity to decide what data they can see and modify. Without auth, every user sees every task — or worse, can delete them.

teach.title: Auth Is Like a Hotel Key Card
teach.body: Imagine a hotel with no room keys. Anyone could walk into any room, take your stuff, or sleep in your bed. Authentication is the key card — it proves which room is yours. Authorization (a related concept) is what the key card unlocks: your room, the pool, but not the staff office.
Authentication verifies identity ('who are you?'), authorization controls access ('what can you do?'). Any app that stores user-specific data needs both.

cards.content: Without authentication, any visitor to your app could view, edit, or delete any user's tasks
cards.explanation: Correct. Without auth, there's no way to distinguish between users, so all data is essentially public to anyone who finds the URL.

cards.content: You only need authentication if your app handles payments
cards.explanation: Any app with user-specific data needs auth — not just payment apps. A task manager stores personal tasks that shouldn't be visible to strangers.

cards.content: Authentication should be added early in development, not bolted on at the end
cards.explanation: Adding auth later often requires rewriting data models and API routes. Planning for it early saves major refactoring headaches.

cards.content: Storing user passwords in plain text in your database is fine as long as the database is private
cards.explanation: Never store passwords in plain text. Databases get breached. Always hash passwords with a strong algorithm like bcrypt. Many breaches expose plain-text passwords because developers assumed their database was safe.

cards.content: Using a third-party auth provider like Supabase Auth or Auth0 is a smart choice for most projects
cards.explanation: Auth providers handle password hashing, session management, OAuth flows, and security updates. Rolling your own auth is error-prone and rarely worth it for most apps.

cards.content: It's safe to check user identity only on the frontend and trust whatever the browser sends to the server
cards.explanation: Frontend checks can be bypassed with browser dev tools. The server must always verify the user's identity independently — never trust the client.
